body: In systems with imbalanced power\‚ credibility isn’t earned - it’s assigned.    Leaders are assumed trustworthy.    Survivors are required to prove harm.    They’re expected to have:  • documented proof  • multiple witnesses  • calm delivery  • no anger\‚ no confusion    Anything else becomes evidence against them.    That isn\\'t transparency\‚ that\\'s power deciding whose story gets believed.    Those with authority are given the benefit of the doubt.    Those harmed are placed under scrutiny.    Survivors are asked:  – Why didn’t you speak up sooner?  – Why didn’t you leave?  – Why didn’t you say no?    Leaders are protected by:  – reputation  – position  – networks  – institutional loyalty    Their harm is reframed as misunderstanding.    The survivor’s response is reframed as instability.    This is how systems avoid accountability without directly saying “we don’t believe you.”    They say:  • “We need more clarity”  • “There are two sides”  • “We can’t act without certainty”    When credibility is tied to hierarchy instead of protecting the vulnerable\‚ harm doesn’t just go unaddressed - it’s repeated.    Survivors learn quickly: Silence is safer than disclosure\‚ and compliance is safer than honesty.    If a system truly protects the vulnerable\‚ belief is not determined by power.    ❤️‍🩹 Until then\‚ survivors aren’t just surviving harm\‚ they’re carrying the burden of proving they deserve to be believed.    If you had to \\prove\\" your harm before you were believed & taken seriously\‚ I see you\‚ & you deserve so much better!    #religioustrauma #cults #churchtoo #metoo #coercivecontrol"

body: ⚖️ 97% of r@pists won’t spend a single day in jail. That’s not justice. That’s a cover up    This isn’t a glitch. It’s the default. When 97% of r@pists walk free\‚ it tells survivors that speaking up changes nothing. That silence is safer than truth. And that’s not just a legal failure; it’s a cultural one. We protect perpetrators more than we protect people who survive them. This number speaks loud and clear. And it says “You’re on your own”     But we’re NOT staying quiet anymore. Justice doesn’t mean punishment. It means protection\‚ belief and a system that values human dignity over power. body: Last week\‚ two members of Congress resigned after facing sexual misconduct accusations. California Democratic Rep. Eric Swalwell dropped out of the race for governor\‚ and Republican Rep. Tony Gonzales of Texas resigned after a bipartisan effort to hold him accountable and expel him from office.     🎞️ @cheyenne.l.hunt    Rep. Cory Mills remains in office despite an active Ethics Committee investigation into sexual misconduct and violence against an ex-girlfriend. Meanwhile\‚ Melania Trump hand-picked director Brett Ratner — accused of sexual misconduct by six women — to helm her Amazon documentary.    Republicans in Washington have come under increased scrutiny following this wave of resignations for how swiftly and vocally they called for the resignation of Swalwell\‚ a Democrat\‚ after months of silence on the Epstein files\‚ which have still not been released in full.     Sexual misconduct is a rampant issue in D.C.\‚ and one of the barriers to women and gender expansive people entering politics. The #MeToo movement previously caused a wave of reckoning for powerful men\‚ including those in Congress\‚ but swift backlash to #MeToo\‚ including Trump taking office in 2016\‚ posed challenges to the movement. Now\‚ as survivors continue to bravely speak up\‚ the pressure to hold politicians accountable is mounting once again.     Source: The 19th News     TAKE ACTION:   👉 Sexual violence can happen to anyone and is NEVER the victim-survivor’s fault. For anyone who may need help\‚ find resources: @metoomvmt @rainn @niceforwhatmvmt @strongheartsdv   👉 U.S. National Sexual Assault hotline: 800-656-4673  👉 StrongHearts Native Helpline: 844-7 762-8483  👉 If you have a story you’re exploring coming forward with find resources via lawyer\‚ organizer & activist Cheyenne Hunt: @cheyenne.l.hunt  👉 Stay informed. body: Misogyny creates abusive environments. Environments where women and children are taken advantage of. Where men misuse their power and greater physical strength to fulfill their ego needs and provide momentary pleasure. The men (and yes\‚ some women) who create these abusive environments do not care about the damage or the trauma they cause.     Churches\‚ organizations\‚ workplaces\‚ family systems\‚ etc. that silence\‚ marginalize\‚ and/or exclude women leave women and children more vulnerable to abuse. If you’re a man in a position of authority\‚ what are you doing right now to change this? How are you standing with and defending those who have been harmed by abuse? You can help to stop this\‚ but only if you speak up and risk being misunderstood or marginalized yourself. If we hope to end abuse and the other effects of misogyny\‚ we need men to be allies\‚ advocates\‚ and interruptors. Please read For the Love of Women: Uprooting and Healing Misogyny in America for more. Visuals by the talented and generous @corriespondent If this resonates with you plz share the post with your people.   #metoo  #churchtoo  #sexims  #Epsteinfiles  #Misogyny  womenauthors  @zondervanreflective

body: The hardest part of seeking justice was not telling my story (that was hard don\\'t get me wrong\‚ but that\\'s not the point I want to make).  It was learning that the system was never built to hold it gently.    In court\‚ survivors are often reduced to witnesses — participants in a process rather than people living with the aftermath of violence. Cross-examination is designed to test evidence\‚ yet it can feel like survival itself is being questioned.    In May 2023\‚ I sat on the stand and experienced that reality first-hand. Words were placed into my mouth.   My memory was challenged repeatedly.   I had prepared myself for difficulty\‚ but preparation could not soften the experience of being dismantled while the man who harmed me watched from inside his glass box and my parents who were sat close by in the gallery.    I lost composure.   I directly called him out\‚ stood\‚ pointed in his direction and shouted a statement I won\\'t repeat; through the court.   He made eye contact and smirked.  Panic took over.     Court paused while I was placed in a small room to gather myself before returning to finish giving evidence. I apologised to the judge and jury for my reaction — a moment that now feels painfully symbolic of how survivors are expected to remain composed while reliving trauma.    These experiences are not isolated incidents.   Delayed cases\‚ victim blaming\‚ institutional doubt\‚ and re-traumatisation remain embedded within a system many enter believing will bring closure.    Speaking about these red flags is not an attack on justice. I promise.  It is a call to examine how justice is delivered — and who must endure the greatest cost to pursue it.    Awareness is not anger.  It is accountability.    To whatever end\‚ still the music plays and #IfItKillsMeITried.  -  #JusticeSystemReform #SurvivorVoices #SpeakYourTruth #MeToo #VictimBlaming #Accountability #LegalSystemAwareness #SA #EndVictimBlaming #ItStopsWithUs #DearSurvivor #RedFlags

body: I had to repost this\‚ by @team.project.medusa …because it is so…oooo TRUE & Relatable(From Experience)…I cried❤️‍🩹! Especially pic 4; “Once you report\‚ it’s NO longer your case”… I did feel like just a piece of evidence…but worse (I never felt protected by the Crown -knowing\‚ it’s not their job to…but a bit of empathy\‚ would’ve been nice). More to the point\‚ All of the other evidence\‚ was more protected than I was. Meaning\‚ the defence would need a court order\‚ for my therapy\‚ medical records & private messages btwn friends\‚ to be used during trial  Also…  Since April is\‚ (SAAM)Sexual Assault Awareness Month\‚ I thought 💭 this would be a great place to start….that “Awareness”!  My experience… left me feeling mislead(lied to)\‚ used/reused\‚ Violated\‚ Abandoned & then\‚ simply Disposed of\‚ by “the system” our “criminal justice system”!   So…  This has to be\‚ THE greatest/closest explanation \‚ I’ve come across(thus far)\‚ depicting my own experience; of our own “Criminal Justice System” & How the justice system is most definitely “NOT” built\‚ for victims/survivors\‚ of any type crime\‚ against them….(sexual assault\‚ specifically for the month of April!💯 #victim #survivor #metoo #sexualassault #sexcrimes   BTW: the Detectives from 52 division\‚ were both trauma informed & handled the investigation with great care…they went “above and beyond” their duties\‚ fighting vigorously in my defence\‚ throughout especially during meetings\‚ with the Crown! So when I say\‚ there’s a problem with our “Criminal Justice System” I am referring to\‚ what happens after the charges are laid\‚ arrests are made & the Crowns decision\‚ to take the case to trial! The Crown & the Law\‚ are where the problem’s lie! It’s almost as if\‚ there’s a “breakdown”; in\‚ (almost what seems\‚ to resemble a “Lapse” in )“chain of custody”\‚ communication &/or (something is lost in translation)\‚ when the TPS\‚ hands over the evidence\‚ they’ve collected to the “crown”!  There really should be\‚ more efforts made\‚ to encourage\‚ interdepartmental communication and cooperative efforts made\‚ by TPS and the Crown to work together. Rather than\‚ just handing over the case/evidence (with little\‚ to no) communication & explanation).

body: Harvey Weinstein\‚ a man accused of sexual assault by more than 100 women saw his 2020 rape conviction overturned this morning.     According to NBC news\‚ “the state Court of Appeals found that the judge in the landmark #MeToo trial prejudiced the former film mogul with improper rulings\‚ including a decision to let women testify about allegations that weren’t part of the case. Attorney Lindsay Goldbrum\‚ who represented six Weinstein accusers\‚ said the decision was a “leap backward for the rule of law” and could deter future sexual assault victims from coming forward. “To all victims of sexual assault who are retraumatized by today’s ruling\‚ I am so sorry\‚” said Goldbrum.”    The Manhattan District Attorney’s Office has released a statement saying it “will do everything in our power to retry this case\‚ and remain steadfast in our commitment to survivors of sexual assault.”    Harvey Weinstein will stay in jail because he had other convictions that for now\‚ have not vanished!    Meanwhile the supreme court justices held a fun debate about how much women should suffer in a case about idaho trying to prevent pregnant women from getting emergency  life-saving care because some republican lawmakers don’t like it. Jessic valenti wrote a crucial substack about it.     Under a federal law called the Emergency Medical Treatment and Active Labor Act\‚ or EMTALA hospitals are mandated to provide emergency care for anyone\‚ but the justices are debating whether this should apply to our most vulnerable: pregnant women. cool!    For more breaking news from a feminist perspective subscribe to my substack.

body: A lot of people don’t realize how much expectation shapes what they believe.    There’s an idea of what a survivor should look like…  how they should act…  how they should feel.    And there’s also an idea of what someone who causes harm should look like.    When reality doesn’t match those expectations\‚  it can shift the focus away from what happened…  and onto whether the person fits the story people expect.    That doesn’t mean people are intentionally dismissive.  But it does mean those assumptions matter.    #SexualAssaultAwarenessMonth  #SAAM2026  #BelieveSurvivors  #MeToo  #SexualViolence
author: applesinwinterfarm

body: Who is it not safe to speak against? Be honest… does the answer change depending on who they are?    In a lot of communities\‚ especially smaller ones\‚ safety isn’t just about what happened. It’s about relationships. Reputation. Who people depend on.    The person everyone likes.  The one who employs half the town.  The one who’s “always been good to people.”  The one with connections.    When someone like that is involved\‚ things can get complicated fast. Not because the harm is less real… but because the social cost of acknowledging it can feel higher.    And that doesn’t just affect one person.  It shapes what others are willing to risk.    What they say.  What they don’t say.  What they decide isn’t worth the fallout.    This isn’t always loud or intentional.  Sometimes it’s subtle.  Sometimes it’s just hesitation.    But it matters.    Because when certain people feel harder to question…  it changes what feels possible for everyone else.    So I’m curious... what makes someone feel “untouchable” where you live?    #SexualAssaultAwarenessMonth  #SAAM2026  #BelieveSurvivors  #MeToo  #SexualViolence

body: #womensday #8marchwomensday We have to keep fighting 🚺💪  Women\\'s and girls\\' rights are facing unprecedented growing threats worldwide\‚ from higher levels of discrimination to weaker legal protections\‚ and less funding for programmes and institutions that support and protect women.  What\\'s happening in America right now is  so sad\‚ it\\'s another step backwards for  women.Not only in America\‚ across the globe many women and girls still face discrimination on the basis of sex and gender. Gender inequality underpins many problems which disproportionately affect women and girls\‚ such as domestic and sexual violence\‚ lower pay\‚ lack of access to education\‚ and inadequate healthcare.  For many years women’s rights movements have fought hard to address this inequality\‚ campaigning to change laws or taking to the streets to demand their rights are respected. And new movements have flourished in the digital age\‚ such as the #MeToo campaign which highlights the prevalence of gender-based violence and sexual harassment.  International Women’s Day is a celebration and a call to action. Demonstrations are planned across the World🚺💪  Women’s rights are human rights!   #Netzfrauen #8m #usa #womensday #womansrights #metoo #frauentag #frauenpower #notrump #noafd #fcknazis #omasgegenrechts #frauen #womans #protest #europe #fight #Mädchen #mujeres

body: 👉 Most people think domestic violence is easy to recognize and assess.     It isn’t.    Especially 👉👉👉 when the abuse is coercive control.    The United Nations has described domestic violence as part of the global “Shadow Pandemic” attributed to COVID 2020.     This carousel explains a cycle survivors have been describing for years:    Trigger 👇  Cognitive dissonance 😳  Coercive control escalation 😱  Interpretation without training 🤓  Credibility inversion 🙃  Institutional harm ‼️  Cycle reinforcement 🔄    When coercive control is misinterpreted\‚ protection can turn into doubt and doubt can turn into risk. ⚠️⛔️    This isn’t just about individual cases.    It’s about how systems interpret evidence.    When we don’t understand coercive control\‚ we overestimate our ability to assess it.     💜💜💜That’s where the Dunning-Kruger effect becomes dangerous in domestic violence contexts.    👉 Survivor fear can be labeled instability.  Documentation can be labeled manipulation.    Protective parenting can be labeled interference.    And children remain exposed to harm.    Understanding this cycle helps explain why the Shadow Pandemic continues\‚ even after disclosure.    👇So the question is:    Are we recognizing coercive control…  or misinterpreting it?    Confidence is not expertise.  Interpretation determines protection. body: Survivor-informed journalism is important because…  - The way we talk about power-based violence matters.  - The weaponization of survivor stories online is a crisis and should be treated as such.  - Changing the narratives can disrupt the culture and end the cycle of violence.    Unethical journalism in survivor storytelling:  - Creates more obstacles to support\‚ justice\‚ and care for survivors.  - Reinforces biases and narratives that hurt victims in every community.  - Increases victim blaming and reduces opportunities for accountability and transformation.  - Influences juries\‚ service providers\‚ and decision-makers to have a bias against survivors.  -Undermines victim’s rights and credibility.  Profits from mis/disinformation.    Join us @survivorstoriesdeservebetter. I have had support using EMDR therapy.    I\\'d love to hear if you have been working on emotional healing\‚ or if you would like to know more about how to get started. body: Tarana Burke sat down with The 19th’s Errin Haines last week during South by Southwest for a discussion about the state of diversity\‚ equity and inclusion (DEI) initiatives across the country and the conservative right’s effort to dismantle them.    Burke\‚ an activist and author based in New York City\‚ spearheaded the #MeToo movement in the early aughts and led the social movement that encouraged survivors of sexual abuse to share their stories. In 2017\‚ advocates used #MeToo more than 19 million times on Twitter.    Historically\‚ Republican lawmakers have worked to eliminate DEI initiatives and language because of their so-called “woke” ideologies. During President Trump’s first few days in office\‚ he released a series of executive orders that called for government agencies to shutter their DEI offices\‚ eliminate positions and end trainings immediately.    When asked what concerns her the most about the conservative right’s fight to abandon DEI efforts\‚ Burke responded: “I think the reason why it’s so easy for people to release DEI is because they were never invested in our humanity to start with.”    She continued: “The lack of community that is unfolding in front of our eyes and this deep hatred and vitriol that we’re experiencing has been building and building over time. I’m really unnerved by this lack of humanity.”    ✍️: Eden Turner\‚ reporting fellow  📹: Wynton Wong\‚ multimedia events producer

body: In August 2018\‚ as the #MeToo movement spread across social media and women around the world demanded justice from sexual predators\‚ Michael Wolff\‚ a journalist\‚ forwarded Jeffrey Epstein a plea for help. Wolff wanted Epstein to support Stephen Elliott\‚ a writer looking to sue the creator of the Shitty Media Men List\‚ a crowd-sourced Google Doc that detailed anonymous allegations of misconduct against dozens of men who worked in the media industry.    “I have always thought that the way back from this climate is through specific instances of individuals successfully challenging their persecution\‚” Wolff wrote to Epstein\‚ according to emails released in a tranche from the so-called Epstein files. “If his story is solid he might be worth supporting.”    Initially\‚ Epstein was unmoved. In a single-word\‚ no-punctuation email\‚ the convicted sexual offender replied: “tough.”    “Give it some further thought\‚ if you would\‚” wrote Wolff\‚ who had originally received Elliott’s pitch through Lorin Stein\‚ the former editor of the prestigious Paris Review and another name on the Shitty Media Men List. “I think there is an opening here. What you need is an excuse – or opportunity – to make the public argument.”    Epstein relented: “I’ll help anyway i can. if you like.” #fyp #maga #trump #clinton

body: April is Se*ual Assault Awareness Month    I wanted to explain how Medusa comes into play with this month.   She is a symbol of reclaiming power & strength after trauma especially when it comes to se*ual assault.     In Greek mythology\‚ Medusa was a beautiful priestess that got se*sully assaulted by Poseidon in Athena’s temple. Instead of punishing Poseidon\‚ Athena punished Medusa by turning her into a monster with snakes for hair\‚ transforming her into a victim-blamed survivor whose gaze turned men to stone.     -Victim Blaming & Shaming: The narrative highlights ancient & contemporary victim-blaming\‚ where the woman is held responsible for her own assault\‚ particularly as she was se*ually  assaulted in Athena’s temple.    -Transformation & Trauma: The punishment becoming a monstrous figure capable of turning victims to stone is often interpreted as a visual representation of trauma & the subsequent loss of safety.    -Reclaiming Power: In modern contexts\‚ particularly after the #MeToo movement\‚ Medusa has been recontextualized from a monstrous villain into a survivor. She represents reclaiming the narrative & punishing those who look at her with violent intent.    -Medusa Tattoos: Tattoos of Medusa have become popular as a sign of solidarity among survivors of se*ual abuse\‚ symbolizing their journey from victimhood to strength & protection.    -Mythological Context: Some interpretations of the myth suggest Athena turned Medusa into a monster to protect her by making her a target of fear rather than a target of lust\‚ allowing her to petrify her attackers.     So here it is\‚ the little background of how Medusa is now an important figure for #Metoo.     My love\‚ if one of you is currently going through this here is some important information-   National Se*ual Abuse Prevention #: 1-800- 656- 4673   It is available 24hrs. Here you can share openly & ask questions no matter what you experienced or when it happened. Specialist provide information\‚ resources & referrals to help immediate & long term needs.     Bubblegum & I send all of you lots of love & a tight tight hug. You are not alone 🩷       #doberman #metoo #medusa #sexualassultawarenessmonth

body: It was a wild time growing up in the 90’s especially as a church girl- I grew up with amazing parents but as a whole our generation wasn’t really allowed to ask a lot of questions\‚ to challenge authority or look for deeper context.  .  Questioning our faith or the rituals attached to denominations or cultural trends was seen as disrespectful and it has taken movements such as #churchtoo and #metoo for GenX and Millennial church girls to realize that some of what we adopted as Christ was actually not Jesus at all.  .  I loved growing up in the church but I know a lot of my peers who are just now getting their voices back and to each of you I say\‚ release your story\‚ your sound\‚ your testimony.  .  I’m glad you’re still here fighting for your faith and freedom and clinging to Jesus despite what might have happened to you at the hands of broken people.  .  We need your story of how Jesus has shown up to prove He is Father and friend and Savior and preserver.  You didn’t destruct\‚ you detangled and now we get to see who Jesus really is aside from all the rules and restrictions.  .  He’s for you. He loves us.  He’s never take His eyes off of you.

body: What would you do?     🚨 “82 % of U.S. women and 42 % of men have experienced sexual harassment or assault at some point in their lives – and the rate for women hasn’t budged since 2018.” (Raj et al.\‚ 2024)    📍 For women\‚ 73 % report harassment in public spaces (streets\‚ parks\‚ transit)\‚ while assault more often occurs in private homes.    👧 Over half of women (56 %) and nearly one in five (20 %) report their first harassment or assault experience before age 18 or even before age 13.    🤐 A shocking 87 % of women who experienced harassment or assault in the past 12 months did not talk to anyone about it.    💬 The wince-worthy truth: despite the wider visibility of # MeToo\‚ prevalence hasn’t dropped — meaning awareness alone hasn’t resolved the root problems.    If you’re tired of being told to “ignore it\‚” don’t. body: A years-long\‚ explosive investigation by The New York Times last month exposed allegations of sexual abuse by the late labor leader Cesar Chavez.     Two members of Congress\‚ one Democrat and one Republican\‚ resigned this week after being accused of sexual misconduct.     And Labor Secretary Lori Chavez-DeRemer is facing an internal investigation\‚ including allegations of inappropriate behavior by her father and husband toward young women staff.     The #MeToo movement\‚ founded in 2006 by activist Tarana Burke\‚ brought a reckoning for powerful men at its public peak in 2017 and 2018\‚ leading to changes in laws and policies to address abuse and help survivors get justice.     It also drew a swift cultural backlash\‚ culminating in President Donald Trump’s election to a second term after he was found liable of sexual abuse.     Now\‚ in 2026\‚ sexual misconduct by people in positions of power is back at the forefront of the political conversation and public discourse.     The groundswell of outrage shows people are “fed up\‚” said Sarah Higginbotham\‚ co-founder and co-director of the National Women’s Defense League (NWDL)\‚ which focuses on sexual misconduct at the state level.    “There is currently a survivor and sexual abuse issue that is at the epicenter of national and global politics right now\‚” she added. “I would like to say that I don’t know how much more evidence people need that this is something that should be prioritized.”    Comment “read” and we’ll DM you a 🔗 to the full story.    ✍️: Grace Panetta\‚ politics reporter  📸: Ron Sachs

body: She Risked Everything to Expose Hollywood’s Darkest Secret:-    At the height of her career\‚ Rose McGowan became one of the first actresses to publicly speak out against powerful Hollywood producer Harvey Weinstein. She knew that exposing the abuse and the system protecting it could cost her everything. Soon after\‚ she faced intense backlash\‚ smear campaigns\‚ and was labeled “difficult” and “unstable.” Major opportunities disappeared\‚ and she was effectively blacklisted in Hollywood. Despite losing work and even selling personal assets to fund legal battles\‚ she refused to stay silent. Her courage played a key role in bringing global attention to decades of abuse\‚ contributing to Weinstein’s eventual conviction and imprisonment. Her decision to speak out also helped ignite the worldwide #MeToo movement\‚ forever changing how the entertainment industry handles misconduct. Her story remains a powerful reminder that those who speak the truth first often face the greatest personal consequences\‚ but their bravery can reshape history.    🔖 Hashtags:  🏛️ Video Specific: #RoseMcGowan #HarveyWeinstein #MeTooMovement #HollywoodScandal #SpeakTheTruth #fblifestyle  📚 Profile Specific: #Fact #Facts #DidYouKnow #Trivia #KnowledgeIsPower #LearnSomethingNew #FunFacts #FactOfTheDay #InterestingFacts #FactCheck #FactFinder #AmazingFacts #ScientificFacts #BizarreFacts #TrueStory #FactOrFiction #FactLovers #FactualInformation #Edutainment #Story    This video discusses sensitive topics related to abuse and is intended for awareness and educational purposes. body: The criminal ‘justice’ system is failing survivors👇    According to a study from Tulane University\‚ rates of SA have not declined since the #MeToo Movement. Despite all of the awareness and advocacy raised\‚ the prevalence of s.xual violence & harassment has remained the same 😔     According to a survey with victim advocates\‚ 88% of survivors report being re-traumatized by the court process.     Very few people trust or feel safe enough within the criminal legal system to report. When they do\‚ cases are often dismissed\‚ which is ESPECIALLY true for marginalized folks. In the rare instance that they DO make it to trial\‚ acquittal is still so common. @RAINN reports that 975 out of 1000 perpetrators walk free 🤔    This is just ONE reason why I’m so passionate about advocating for restorative justice & alternatives to the punitive system. Survivors deserve so much better. We deserve a justice pathway that catalyzes our healing.    We deserve trauma-informed care and the recognition that there’s no ‘one size fits all’ when it comes to justice.    I am forever grateful that my SA case set precedent by concluding with restorative justice through the courts. After much fighting\‚ my needs were heard & centred and I recognize the deep privilege in that. We need to centre all survivors voices & ensure that healing and justice are made synonymous.    COMMUNITY DISCUSSION:  ✍️ What are your thought on the criminal ‘justice system’? Do you feel like it truly supports survivors?  ✍️ In a dream world\‚ what would your version of justice look like?    💜 Follow for more on Restorative Justice\‚ survivor led advocacy\‚ trauma-informed pleasure\‚ consent education & violence prevention  ✈️ Share to normalize convo on trauma responses and reclamation based support for survivors  🎤 Visit marleeliss.com/speaking to learn more about my keynote\‚ workshops & training on these topics and more. I’m always honoured to share on healing justice at campuses\‚ conferences & for orgs internationally!

body: This post highlights the courageous role of actress Rose McGowan in dismantling the culture of silence within the film industry. By being one of the first to publicly accuse Harvey Weinstein of sexual assault\‚ she risked her career and faced severe professional retaliation\‚ including being blacklisted and subjected to smear campaigns. The text underscores how her sacrifices were instrumental in catalyzing the global #MeToo movement\‚ ultimately leading to systemic accountability and Weinstein\\'s conviction. It serves as a tribute to the heavy personal cost often borne by whistleblowers who challenge powerful institutions to drive historical change.

body: Before hashtags and press conferences\‚ there was Shirley Temple quietly putting it on the record. In her 1988 memoir Child Star and later interviews\‚ the former box-office phenomenon described what she faced as a girl working in 1930s–40s Hollywood: powerful men who treated access to a child performer as entitlement. She recalled studio “meetings” where an executive asked her mother for “favors” in exchange for roles\‚ and an infamous audition as a young teen at MGM where producer Arthur Freed allegedly exposed himself; when she laughed in shock\‚ the meeting ended and the opportunity vanished.    Temple’s accounts weren’t lurid; they were matter-of-fact. She framed them as warnings about a system that rewarded silence\‚ protected abusers\‚ and placed children in adult spaces with adult stakes. The stories land harder when you remember who’s telling them: the most famous child in America\‚ a figure studios packaged as pure innocence. As she grew out of her ringlets and into adolescence\‚ she met the underside of that mythology—closed doors\‚ whispered conditions\‚ and the constant need for a vigilant parent at her side.    Long before #MeToo\‚ Temple used her platform to name the behavior and to argue for guardrails: chaperones with real authority\‚ transparent auditions\‚ and consequences when lines are crossed. The point of revisiting her testimony isn’t shock; it’s context. The golden age wasn’t golden for everyone\‚ and the industry’s hardest lessons were often learned by its youngest workers. Shirley Temple should be remembered for the joy she brought on screen—and for telling the truth about what it cost off it.

body: April is Sexual Assault Awareness Month. In partnership with Freely In Hope and Love Never Fails\‚ I will be leading a 14-week faith-based healing circle and hosting monthly worship nights for survivors of sexual trauma.     🫶🏼 As an overcomer of child sexual abuse (CSA)\‚ teen rapes\‚ intimate partner violence\‚ drug-induced rapes\‚ sexual coercion\‚ & sex trafficking\‚ it’s ignited my life’s purpose to help fellow survivors heal on their journey. I am so grateful to be entering my 5 year anniversary of facilitating these support groups for survivors💕    This will be a safe space where we can  heal in community and invite God every step of the way♥️ In this collective space\‚ we will get to connect through authentic sharing/games/activities\‚  explore different ways to release trauma trapped in the body\‚ and engage in creative expression (dance/art/writing). Together we give ourselves permission to dream again\‚ rebuild new foundations\‚ and cultivate a life that is aligned to our purpose.    It\\'s time to FLOURISH FORWARD WITH GRACE as we take courageous steps towards healing. Let\\'s build community with fellow survivors...and live a life of healing\‚ restoration\‚ and being set free from the pain of our past.     🫶🏼I BELIEVE YOU.  🫶🏼You are not alone.  🫶🏼Let’s take this next step on this journey together! body: April is Sexual Assault Awareness Month. I had written a post to go along with this video but wound up deciding not to share & deleted it. I know the #metoo movement showed how far-reaching #SA is but this video of all these different audiences showing it in this way really brought it home for me. After seeing that 62 MILLION men were on the “Rape Academy” website learning to drug and rape women in more efficient ways I realized I did need to speak out.     If you are a man this “not all men” BS has to STOP. If you are an ally to women\‚ now is the time to actually speak out about it — tell your fellow men how you feel. Let us know we have support from men in the world. We as women have felt unsafe around men our whole lives\‚ we were/are not wrong.     I have personally experienced sexual assault numerous times in my lifetime\‚ including but not limited to being molested by my mother’s boyfriend as a child\‚ and being raped twice later\‚ one of which occurred when I was 14 years old and still a virgin. I will not be seen as a victim and do not feel like one but when I see 62M men trying to learn how to more efficiently hurt women\‚ I have to speak out. I had a thoughtful conversation with a tattoo artist yesterday that also made me want to send some support out to the women who need to hear this: YOU ARE NOT ALONE AND IT WAS NOT YOUR FAULT. ❤️‍🩹    Unfortunately\‚ a little known fact is that being sexually assaulted puts you at a much higher risk of being assaulted again in the future\‚ as does childhood sexual abuse. Being sexually assaulted so greatly increased the risk of future assaults\‚ one study purported that being sexually assaulted once meant a woman was 35 times more likely than others to be revictimized. 💔    I am going to post something directly after this by a MAN @stefanossifandos who I was really impressed by\‚ saying the things you #notallmen “men” should be saying. THIS is what a real man sounds like\‚ not some cowardly creep looking on the internet for more tips and strategies to rape and harm women.    “Men are afraid that women will laugh at them. body: The recent resignations of two members of Congress have reignited a conversation about sexual harassment in politics nearly nine years after the peak of the #MeToo movement. And new data sheds more light on the scope of the problem — and the major barriers to reporting and addressing abuse.     The National Women’s Defense League (NWDL)\‚ a nonpartisan\‚ nonprofit organization focused on combating sexual misconduct in politics at the state level\‚ released its annual updated report detailing ongoing research into sexual misconduct in statehouses and — for the first time — data on sexual misconduct in Congress on Tuesday.     The 2025 update to NWDL’s Abuse of Power Report tracking sexual misconduct in statehouses and state executive offices\‚ identified 162 state-level officials accused of misconduct in 424 distinct incidents since 2013.     That includes six new public allegations of sexual misconduct made against state-level officials in 2025\‚ a decline in reporting from previous years.    “We’ve got enough data that we’re separated from the #MeToo spike and then the pandemic to where we can confidently say we’ve really gone back to pre-#MeToo levels in terms of levels of reporting\‚” said Emma Davidson Tribbs\‚ NWDL’s founding director. “And that reinforces\‚ I think\‚ what we’re seeing nationally\‚ which is the discomfort and fear of survivors coming forward.”    Comment “read” and we’ll DM you a 🔗 to the full story.    ✍️: Grace Panetta\‚ politics reporter  📸: Leigh Vogel/Getty Images

body: In systems with imbalanced power\‚ credibility isn’t earned - it’s assigned.    Leaders are assumed trustworthy.    Survivors are required to prove harm.    They’re expected to have:  • documented proof  • multiple witnesses  • calm delivery  • no anger\‚ no confusion    Anything else becomes evidence against them.    That isn\\'t transparency\‚ that\\'s power deciding whose story gets believed.    Those with authority are given the benefit of the doubt.    Those harmed are placed under scrutiny.    Survivors are asked:  – Why didn’t you speak up sooner?  – Why didn’t you leave?  – Why didn’t you say no?    Leaders are protected by:  – reputation  – position  – networks  – institutional loyalty    Their harm is reframed as misunderstanding.    The survivor’s response is reframed as instability.    This is how systems avoid accountability without directly saying “we don’t believe you.”    They say:  • “We need more clarity”  • “There are two sides”  • “We can’t act without certainty”    When credibility is tied to hierarchy instead of protecting the vulnerable\‚ harm doesn’t just go unaddressed - it’s repeated.    Survivors learn quickly: Silence is safer than disclosure\‚ and compliance is safer than honesty.    If a system truly protects the vulnerable\‚ belief is not determined by power.    ❤️‍🩹 Until then\‚ survivors aren’t just surviving harm\‚ they’re carrying the burden of proving they deserve to be believed.    If you had to \\prove\\" your harm before you were believed & taken seriously\‚ I see you\‚ & you deserve so much better!    #religioustrauma #cults #churchtoo #metoo #coercivecontrol"

body: Cameron Diaz finds it easier to be a woman in Hollywood since returning to acting.⁠  ⁠  The ‘Back In Action’ star – who stepped away from the spotlight for about a decade --credits the #MeToo movement for making film sets a better working environment.⁠  ⁠  “You walk onto the set and it is different\‚” the mother of two said on the ‘SkipIntro’ podcast. “…There was always just that one guy\‚ you know\‚ on set\‚ that you were like\‚ ‘God\‚ here he comes again.’ There were always layers and layers of inappropriateness that you just kind of had to put up with. As women\‚ you just had to like [laugh it off].”⁠  ⁠  Cameron says she’s impressed with how Netflix handles things. “And there’s a hotline\‚ which Netflix has\‚ to call anonymously to report any issues that you might be feeling. Wow\‚ that is amazing\‚” she shared. “The level of security of safety you feel as a woman now on set is. …I never felt that before this film.” (📸: Getty Images)

body: This week\‚ the daughter of the acclaimed short-story writer Alice Munro revealed that her stepfather had molested her when she was a child\‚ and that Munro\‚ who died in May\‚ had remained married to him even after learning the truth. How\‚ Munro’s many fans are no doubt asking themselves\‚ can they still read and admire her stories?    “The #MeToo movement has given us one answer to the question of how to manage art we love after it has been recategorized as the output of a morally ‘bad actor.’ We throw it out\‚” Xochitl Gonzalez writes. “There are now scores of films we should not watch\‚ albums we should not stream\‚ and brands we should not wear.”    “In the beginning this was\‚ I admit\‚ a thrilling act\‚” Gonzalez continues. “We were invigorated by seizing our power as consumers of art and devaluing perpetrators with so much wealth and fame and glory. But this reaction was not only culturally immature; it was also unsustainable. At this rate\‚ we risk throwing away the art and culture that define us.”    Genius can have a cascading effect\‚ Gonzalez argues; if someone has that much talent\‚ they must have a corresponding number of virtues. “And because Munro was a woman\‚ that virtue extends to our presumptions of her as a mother (perhaps one of the few benefits of sexism). We seem unable to imagine that someone who was such a good writer could be a bad mother. But it is our very investment in that cascade of genius\‚ and in the idea of the virtuous artist\‚ that has protected so many predators. Of the many outrages in this story\‚ one of the most upsetting is that the family stayed silent in part because they felt an obligation to preserve Munro’s reputation. This happens all the time\‚ though most members of the protected class of genius have been men\‚ whose misdeeds must be kept behind locked doors because there is—always—‘too much at stake.’”    “If we don’t shun the work\‚ how will we punish the artist?” Gonzalez continues at the link in our bio. “That’s the wrong question to ask. For one thing\‚ Munro is dead and doesn’t care anymore what we think. But even for artists who are alive and well\‚ the more effective response is to stop putting them on pedestals in the first place.”

body: Survivor-informed journalism is important because…  - The way we talk about power-based violence matters.  - The weaponization of survivor stories online is a crisis and should be treated as such.  - Changing the narratives can disrupt the culture and end the cycle of violence.    Unethical journalism in survivor storytelling:  - Creates more obstacles to support\‚ justice\‚ and care for survivors.  - Reinforces biases and narratives that hurt victims in every community.  - Increases victim blaming and reduces opportunities for accountability and transformation.  - Influences juries\‚ service providers\‚ and decision-makers to have a bias against survivors.  -Undermines victim’s rights and credibility.  Profits from mis/disinformation.    Join us @survivorstoriesdeservebetter.     Image descriptions: Maroon background with text above across four slides.     #SurvivorStoriesDeserveBetter #EthicalJournalism #MeToo #SurvivorInformedJournalism #TraumaResponsive

body: I had to repost this\‚ by @team.project.medusa …because it is so…oooo TRUE & Relatable(From Experience)…I cried❤️‍🩹! Especially pic 4; “Once you report\‚ it’s NO longer your case”… I did feel like just a piece of evidence…but worse (I never felt protected by the Crown -knowing\‚ it’s not their job to…but a bit of empathy\‚ would’ve been nice). More to the point\‚ All of the other evidence\‚ was more protected than I was. Meaning\‚ the defence would need a court order\‚ for my therapy\‚ medical records & private messages btwn friends\‚ to be used during trial  Also…  Since April is\‚ (SAAM)Sexual Assault Awareness Month\‚ I thought 💭 this would be a great place to start….that “Awareness”!  My experience… left me feeling mislead(lied to)\‚ used/reused\‚ Violated\‚ Abandoned & then\‚ simply Disposed of\‚ by “the system” our “criminal justice system”!   So…  This has to be\‚ THE greatest/closest explanation \‚ I’ve come across(thus far)\‚ depicting my own experience; of our own “Criminal Justice System” & How the justice system is most definitely “NOT” built\‚ for victims/survivors\‚ of any type crime\‚ against them….(sexual assault\‚ specifically for the month of April!💯 #victim #survivor #metoo #sexualassault #sexcrimes   BTW: the Detectives from 52 division\‚ were both trauma informed & handled the investigation with great care…they went “above and beyond” their duties\‚ fighting vigorously in my defence\‚ throughout especially during meetings\‚ with the Crown! So when I say\‚ there’s a problem with our “Criminal Justice System” I am referring to\‚ what happens after the charges are laid\‚ arrests are made & the Crowns decision\‚ to take the case to trial! The Crown & the Law\‚ are where the problem’s lie! It’s almost as if\‚ there’s a “breakdown”; in\‚ (almost what seems\‚ to resemble a “Lapse” in )“chain of custody”\‚ communication &/or (something is lost in translation)\‚ when the TPS\‚ hands over the evidence\‚ they’ve collected to the “crown”!  There really should be\‚ more efforts made\‚ to encourage\‚ interdepartmental communication and cooperative efforts made\‚ by TPS and the Crown to work together. Rather than\‚ just handing over the case/evidence (with little\‚ to no) communication & explanation).

body: In August 2018\‚ as the #MeToo movement spread across social media and women around the world demanded justice from sexual predators\‚ Michael Wolff\‚ a journalist\‚ forwarded Jeffrey Epstein a plea for help. Wolff wanted Epstein to support Stephen Elliott\‚ a writer looking to sue the creator of the Shitty Media Men List\‚ a crowd-sourced Google Doc that detailed anonymous allegations of misconduct against dozens of men who worked in the media industry.    “I have always thought that the way back from this climate is through specific instances of individuals successfully challenging their persecution\‚” Wolff wrote to Epstein\‚ according to emails released in a tranche from the so-called Epstein files. “If his story is solid he might be worth supporting.”    Initially\‚ Epstein was unmoved. In a single-word\‚ no-punctuation email\‚ the convicted sexual offender replied: “tough.”    “Give it some further thought\‚ if you would\‚” wrote Wolff\‚ who had originally received Elliott’s pitch through Lorin Stein\‚ the former editor of the prestigious Paris Review and another name on the Shitty Media Men List. “I think there is an opening here. What you need is an excuse – or opportunity – to make the public argument.”    Epstein relented: “I’ll help anyway i can. if you like.” #fyp #maga #trump #clinton

body: Almost half of Gen Z men think that “women’s right have gone far enough” - according to a new Channel 4 report being released this Thursday.    45% of men aged 13-27 agree that “we have gone so far in promoting women’s equality that we are discriminating against men’”    While in another leaked report\‚ The Guardian today reported that misogyny & the manosphere has been identified as breeding ground for extremism in UK    We *know* extreme misogyny is on the rise.    Yet the Home Office has announced there are no plans to expand the definition of extremism\‚ following reports it could be broadened to cover potentially violent environmentalists and misogynists.    The failure to call extreme misogyny what it is\‚ only does a disservice to past\‚ curent and future victims of this ideology. We know where this ends.     It not only harms women and people of marginalised genders\‚ it pigeonholes\‚ restricts the lives and damages men too.     #misogyny #endmisogyny #callitwhatitis #metoohasnotgonefarenough

body: Dylan Farrow recounts the traumatic experience of being assaulted by her father\‚ Woody Allen\‚ as a seven-year-old\‚ saying he touched my private parts. This phrase is a stark reminder of the abuse she suffered. The conversation reveals a disturbing pattern of behavior\‚ with Dylan Farrow stating that her father would often ask her to get into bed with him when he had only his underwear on.    The video sheds light on the emotional turmoil Dylan Farrow faced after the incident\‚ with her initially thinking she had done something wrong. The conversation also highlights the importance of her mother\\'s support\‚ who encouraged her to tell the truth throughout the ordeal. As the conversation progresses\‚ Dylan Farrow shares more details about the abuse\‚ including the fact that her father would sometimes be in his underwear when asking her to get into bed with him. The on-screen text\‚ which includes the phrase sorry Dylan Farrow was assaulted by her father Woody Allen\‚ serves as a stark reminder of the horrific experience she endured. The visual context of a distressed woman holding a girl near a brick wall adds to the emotional impact of the conversation.    The conversation is a powerful testament to Dylan Farrow\\'s courage in sharing her story\‚ and it raises important questions about the handling of abuse cases. As Dylan Farrow reflects on her experience\‚ it becomes clear that her mother\\'s support played a crucial role in her ability to speak out. The conversation ultimately leaves the viewer with a sense of sadness and outrage\‚ prompting us to think about how we can create a safer and more supportive environment for survivors of abuse. What can we learn from Dylan Farrow\\'s story\‚ and how can we work towards creating a world where such tragedies are prevented?    #childabuse #metoo #reels    Follow @stelle.world for tech\‚ science and everything in between. Even gruesome facts which we wish never took place.

body: Price:₹350+shipping    How dysfunction\‚ sexual misconduct and scandal almost brought down one of Hollywood’s greatest companies.    Unscripted is the inside story of the struggle to control one of the world’s great entertainment empires.    It is the story of the last great Hollywood mogul\‚ Sumner Redstone: the ninety-something founder of Paramount Global who\‚ well into his dotage and facing a scandalous lawsuit\‚ proves increasingly unable to run the sprawling company he has built.    It is the story of his daughter\‚ Shari Redstone: Sumner’s heir apparent who\‚ despite being groomed for power for six decades\‚ struggles to assert her authority over the company and her family’s legacy.    And it is the story of her challenger\‚ Leslie Moonves: the well-liked CEO of CBS who plots a coup to take control of the business - until news leaks that he is facing multiple allegations of sexual misconduct (allegations he has spent years trying to hush up).    The result is damning portrait of how money and power works in Hollywood now. It illuminates an industry struggling to adapt to the revolution brought by streaming\‚ #MeToo and Black Lives Matter. And it reveals the lengths people will go to in pursuit of power - and the carnage that ensues when they do.

body: #MeTooFamilyCourt  proudly supports @drjesstaylor and a “Call for Evidence”    Share if you do\‚ too.    🫵 #MeToo #FamilyCourt    #LoveMom #IMissYou    Get @reshare_app • @drjesstaylor CALL FOR EVIDENCE     The world needs powerful\‚ anti-pathology research and evidence bases. So together\‚ @jaimi_shrive and I founded IITAS.     IITAS is the International Institute for Trauma\‚ Abuse and Society - we are also over at @iitas_institute on Instagram.     People who have been subjected to abuse and violence around the world\‚ and then framed as mentally ill - need to be able to share their experiences\‚ ideas\‚ and the impact it has had on their lives.     We then need to be able to share those findings\‚ research and education for free to as many people as possible - in order to truly invest in and develop new anti-pathology\‚ trauma-informed approaches to all forms of abuse\‚ violence\‚ oppression\‚ discrimination\‚ harm\‚ and trauma.     We have two new studies open for participants on our website - where you can take part anonymously from anywhere in the world.     CALL FOR EVIDENCE - TAKE PART     The first study is exploring experiences of mental health support during and after any form of abuse\‚ violence\‚ stalking or coercive control. This is open to anyone over the age of 18 who has ever been through any form of abuse or violence.     The second study is specifically for women who have been through any form of family court proceedings or criminal court proceedings\‚ specifically where their abuse\‚ mental health or trauma was cited\‚ discussed or assessed.     You can take part on the website under the ‘Take Part’ tab.     If you would like to learn more or follow our findings and reports\‚ you can also follow and tag our new instagram @iitas_institute

body: 👉 Most people think domestic violence is easy to recognize and assess.     It isn’t.    Especially 👉👉👉 when the abuse is coercive control.    The United Nations has described domestic violence as part of the global “Shadow Pandemic” attributed to COVID 2020.     This carousel explains a cycle survivors have been describing for years:    Trigger 👇  Cognitive dissonance 😳  Coercive control escalation 😱  Interpretation without training 🤓  Credibility inversion 🙃  Institutional harm ‼️  Cycle reinforcement 🔄    When coercive control is misinterpreted\‚ protection can turn into doubt and doubt can turn into risk. ⚠️⛔️    This isn’t just about individual cases.    It’s about how systems interpret evidence.    When we don’t understand coercive control\‚ we overestimate our ability to assess it.     💜💜💜That’s where the Dunning-Kruger effect becomes dangerous in domestic violence contexts.    👉 Survivor fear can be labeled instability.  Documentation can be labeled manipulation.    Protective parenting can be labeled interference.    And children remain exposed to harm.    Understanding this cycle helps explain why the Shadow Pandemic continues\‚ even after disclosure.    👇So the question is:    Are we recognizing coercive control…  or misinterpreting it?    Confidence is not expertise.  Interpretation determines protection.    Are you part of the problem or ARE YOU PART TO THE SOLUTION?    #MeTooFamilyCourt    💜    #ShadowPandemic #CoerciveControl #DomesticViolenceAwareness #SURVIVOR

body: (Inspired by @therealhadassa vulnerability. )Those close to me urged me against talking about my journey with sexual abuse. You’ll never get married. What if that becomes your entire personality? Do you want to air your dirty laundry? When October 7th happened and the chronic illness in my body flared up. I was told\‚ “it’s not the same.” You’ll seem insensitive. Don’t talk about it. Yet\‚ my inbox became flooded with survivors unable to function. Women expressing gratitude. Sexual abuse stays in our body because it is associated with shame. It’s shameful to talk about. Getting punched in the face. Not shameful. Getting in a car accident. Not shameful. Going through any different kind of trauma. Violence. Displacement. Dysfunction. Why is sexual abuse different? Is it because of the deeply ingrained patriarchal system we live in? Centuries of silencing women? It is women’s month. Esther’s month. Let’s start painting our Jewish sisters as warriors instead of victims. Let’s greet the 19 women we still have in captivity with awe. Grace. Love. Because this is the month of miracles. Our warriors must come home. Every woman retraumatized by these events- I see you. I hear you. I validate you. You are not alone. #metoo #womensmonth

body: Timothée Chalamet was \\'made to give away his money\‚\\' publicist claims in newly released Epstein files    The pristine image of Hollywood’s golden boy has been called into question after millions of unsealed Department of Justice documents revealed the \\'calculated\\' PR machinery behind his 2018 moral stand. In the latest batch of Jeffrey Epstein files released in February 2026\‚ a series of emails between Epstein and high-level crisis managers suggest that Chalamet’s decision to donate his entire salary from the Woody Allen film \\'A Rainy Day in New York\\' wasn\\'t a spontaneous act of conscience. According to the files\‚ a prominent publicist claimed the actor was essentially \\'made\\' to give the money away to the Time’s Up and RAINN charities to prevent his career from being \\'extinguished\\' by the #MeToo movement before it truly began.    The documents\‚ which show Epstein acting as a back-channel advisor to men accused of misconduct\‚ include discussions on how to \\'neutralize\\' the backlash surrounding Allen’s project. While Chalamet won global praise at the time for stating he \\'did not want to profit\\' from the film\‚ the unsealed records paint a more cynical picture of behind-the-scenes panic. Critics are now debating whether the move was a genuine change of heart or a cold\‚ strategic maneuver dictated by a PR team desperate to protect his Oscar-bound trajectory. As the 2026 \\'Epstein Reckoning\\' continues to expose the dark underbelly of Hollywood power-broking\‚ fans are left wondering how many other \\'heroic\\' celebrity moments were actually drafted by crisis consultants in a billionaire\\'s inbox.

body: ⚖️ 97% of r@pists won’t spend a single day in jail. That’s not justice. That’s a cover up    This isn’t a glitch. It’s the default. When 97% of r@pists walk free\‚ it tells survivors that speaking up changes nothing. That silence is safer than truth. And that’s not just a legal failure; it’s a cultural one. We protect perpetrators more than we protect people who survive them. This number speaks loud and clear. And it says “You’re on your own”     But we’re NOT staying quiet anymore. Justice doesn’t mean punishment. It means protection\‚ belief and a system that values human dignity over power. body: The Diddy trial feels like the trial not of one man\‚ but of something much larger\‚ Xochitl Gonzalez writes. A jury made up of eight men and four women will decide whether to convict Diddy—whose legal name is Sean Combs—“but the broader culture\‚ in its response to this trial\‚ is deciding whether #MeToo was a movement or a moment.”    Combs has pleaded not guilty to the charges of racketeering conspiracy and sex trafficking. Yet “despite the video evidence of domestic violence … and the multiple witnesses testifying that Combs threatened to kill them\‚” Gonzalez writes\‚ many Americans have taken to the comment sections to proclaim that Diddy’s biggest sin is nothing more than being a hypermasculine celebrity with “libertine” sexual tastes. “The jury has already watched the now-infamous surveillance footage of Combs dragging Cassie Ventura\‚ the prosecution’s star witness\‚ by the collar of her sweatshirt through a hotel—and that’s not even one of the things he’s on trial for.”    “At the center of the trial is the question of coercion\‚” Gonzalez continues. Did Cassie Ventura participate in hundreds of drug-fueled sexual encounters with strangers for Combs because she enjoyed them? Or did Combs use his power over her to force her? “The evidence presented by both sides serves as a Rorschach test. How you see it says a lot about how the #MeToo movement did—or did not—alter your vision.”    It’s been almost eight years since the #MeToo movement forced a reassessment of abuse and power. “In the future\‚ I remember thinking\‚ we will not just speak out against bad actors; we will refuse to participate in the systems that protected them\‚” Gonzalez writes. “Everyone would understand that\‚ in a world of power imbalances\‚ the difference between what a woman chooses and what happens to her can be very big indeed.”    Instead\‚ “American women have seen our rights eroded and our access to lifesaving health care curbed. An accused sexual abuser is president of the United States … Many men have fought hard to undermine the progress of the #MeToo movement. Like Combs running after Ventura in that video\‚ they have tried to drag women back into the past\‚ where they could do as they liked.”

body: Hudson Williams is a Canadian actor who grew up in Kamloops\‚ British Columbia. He was raised by a Korean mother and a father of British and Dutch descent\‚ and developed an early interest in film and performance while growing up in Canada. Williams later moved to Vancouver to pursue acting more seriously and completed the Film Arts program at Langara College\‚ graduating in 2020.    He began his career with roles in short films and supporting appearances in television movies and series\‚ gradually building experience across a range of genres. His early screen work included appearances in series such as Tracker and television films including Nobody Dumps My Daughter\‚ as well as the feature film Allegiance. body: 🏹 They’ve called us unstable\‚ intense\‚ and “unfiltered.”    They’ve mistaken our need for expansion for a fear of commitment. Being a Sagittarius isn’t just about blind optimism; it’s having the absolute certainty that there is always a broader horizon.    We don’t run because we are in a hurry; we run because we feel life is too short to stay in places (or with people) that we\\'ve outgrown.    Our honesty isn’t cruelty; it’s the purest form of respect. I tell you the truth because I believe you are strong enough to handle it. If that scares you\‚ then you aren\\'t ready for the ride.    The Centaur is half instinct\‚ half wisdom. If you ignore one half\‚ you will never truly understand us.    👇 Does your fire need direction today?    Sometimes\‚ so many arrows and so much horizon can drain us. body: 日本利用压电瓷砖将脚步转化为电能。这些瓷砖捕捉来自你脚步的动能。当你行走时，你的重量和动作会对瓷砖产生压力。瓷砖会轻微弯曲，从而产生机械应力。瓷砖内部的压电材料将这种应力转化为电能。每一步都会产生少量电荷，而数百万步结合在一起就能产生足够的电力来驱动 LED 灯、数字显示屏和传感器。在像涩谷车站这样繁忙的地方，每天大约有 240 万个脚步为这一系统作出贡献。这些电能可以被储存或立即使用，从而减少对传统电力来源的依赖，并支持可持续的城市基础设施。这种方法将日常运动转化为实用的可再生能源 #日本 #知识 #事实 #你知道吗

body: Survivors aren’t quiet. They’re cautious.    When police don’t believe you…  When Crown counsel drops the charges…  When protection orders aren’t enforced…  When reporting means you risk arrest\‚ losing your kids\‚ or being retraumatized…    Reporting doesn’t feel safe.    Yet survivors are constantly told—by governments\‚ by the public\‚ by media—to “just report it.”  But what’s missing from that message?  Everything about what actually happens after.    That’s why BWSS is seeking the full implementation of The British Columbia Legal System’s  Treatment of Intimate Partner Violence and Sexual Violence by Dr. Kim Stanton    BWSS agrees with most of the 21 recommendations including:    ✔️ the creation of strong internal government accountability mechanism  ✔️ prioritizing prevention initiatives\‚ including public education\‚ to reduce and prevent violence before it escalates to the point of engaging the legal system  ✔️ strengthen and support community-based support workers  ✔️ increasing Family Law survivor support services  ✔️ Mandating all system players to conduct risk assessments with perpetrators of domestic or intimate partner violence    If you’re in an abusive relationship and don’t feel safe going to police\‚ you’re not alone. Call us\‚ we believe you and we’ll walk beside you.    📞 Crisis Line: 1-855-687-1868  🔗 bwss.org/endingviolence    #WhyIDidntReport #DesignedWithSurvivors #StantonReport #GBVEpidemic #endvaw

body: Why most Domestic & S.xual Vi.lence Survivors Don’t Report ❤️‍🩹    Since 2018\‚ the hashtag #WhyIDidntReport has been used on social media more than one million times. Amidst a climate of victim-blaming and skepticism towards victim credibility\‚ survivors courageously share why they didn’t report their experiences to law enforcement.    Few survivors feel safe reporting to police: It’s clear we need alternative options.     We need options for justice after trauma that feel safe\‚ trauma-informed and accessible. We need options that are inclusive and survivor-centred. Restorative and Transformative justice approaches can be an essential pathway for the many victims who do not feel safe\‚ supported or aligned with reporting to law enforcement.    @survivors4justicereform is a global coalition of survivors who stand for justice reform and options beyond the carceral system. We know there is no one size fits all when it comes to justice 🌎    ✍️ Most importantly\‚ fill out the form via the link in @survivors4justicereform bio to join the coalition

body: On unreported cases — I really want to share this project again today.    I visualized the data emerging for the research “Barriers to Reporting Sexual Violence: A Qualitative Analysis of #WhyIDidntReport.” ​There are cases where more than one barrier has been indicated\‚ for this reason the percentages don’t add up to 100%.    “The study conducted thematic analyses of tweets including #WhyIDidntReport to examine barriers to reporting sexual victimization. [...] The hashtag trended in the United States in the  late September 2018. [...] Within 2 days\‚ 675\‚000 tweets trademarked by #WhyIDidntReport were posted on Twitter\‚ describing the reasons why a victim of sexual violence may choose not to report to the police  [...] Barriers to reporting were identified across individual\‚ interpersonal\‚ and sociocultural levels of the social ecology. Common barriers to reporting included labeling of the experience\‚ age\‚ fear\‚ privacy concerns\‚ self-blame\‚ betrayal/shock\‚ the relation/power of the perpetrator\‚ negative reactions to disclosure\‚ and the belief—or personal experience—that reporting would not result in justice and societal norms.     Source: “Barriers to Reporting Sexual Violence: A Qualitative Analysis of #WhyIDidntReport.”\‚ Lindsay M. Orchowski\‚ Lauren Grocott\‚ Katie W. Bogen\‚ Aderonke Ilegbusi\‚ Ananda B. Amstadter\‚ and Nicole R. Nugent.  First published online August 9\‚ 2022.    More methodology information in the comments.    #stopviolenceagainstwomen #patriarchy #25novembre #feminism #25n

body: When people ask\‚ “Why don’t survivors report?”  This is why.    Watch how the system responds.  Watch who gets protected.  Watch how long it takes.  Watch how survivors are questioned\‚ dissected\‚ and doubted — while those in power are given time\‚ space\‚ and benefit of the doubt.    This isn’t new. It’s a pattern.    And survivors are paying attention.    Reporting isn’t just about telling the truth — it’s about stepping into a system that has historically failed to protect us. A system where speaking up can cost you your privacy\‚ your peace\‚ your safety\‚ and sometimes even your credibility.    So when survivors stay silent\‚ it’s not because they don’t want justice.  It’s because they’ve learned what the pursuit of justice can actually look like.    We don’t just need people to “speak up.”  We need systems that are protecting our abusers to protect us.     I never reported a single instance of CSA or SA that I experienced for all of these reasons. And I refuse to feel shame for that any longer.     If you never reported I want you to know you are not alone I see you 🤍    #csasurvivor #sasurvivor #traumahealingjourney #saam #30daysofsaam

body: Actual footage of what it feels like the moment perimenopause/menopause shows up unannounced and uninvited in the lives of neurodivergent folks. Symptoms can feel absolutely unhinged and unbearable\‚ and we need to be talking about this.    This is why @adhdmenopausetherapist\‚ @sarcrasstic.self.care\‚ @hummingbird_adhd and @adhd_with_drjen and I are combing forces to get the word out and support ND folks at the @neurodiversionevent in Austin\‚ TX this weekend in our “Hormones Gone Rogue” expert panel.    We are ridiculously passionate about helping folks find relief\‚ learn how to advocate for themselves\‚ and helping those who are coming up the ranks behind us so they won’t be blindsided in the ways that we were.

body: Start making coffee.See a mug in the sink.Remember I need to text that person back. Pick up phone.See notification about something unrelated. Click it.Forget why I opened my phone.Close phone.Go back to kitchen.What was I doing? Oh right\‚ coffee.Water’s cold now.    And that’s just the first 3 minutes of my day.  Not because I’m not trying. Because my brain processes everything as equally urgent.  ADHD + dysregulated nervous system = every  distraction is amplified    Regulating my nervous system didn’t cure my ADHD.  But it gave me enough capacity to finish making the coffee.  Small wins.    • Comment burnout to get my burnout decoder and get a personalized report on your stress archetype and how to regulate your nervous system

body: The question sounds like concern\‚ but it’s just another way to shift responsibility off the person who caused harm and onto the person who survived it.    I was the victim. I was scared\‚ confused\‚ and trying to make it through something I didn’t have the language\‚ power\‚ or support to even process\‚ let alone report.    The responsibility was never mine. Not then\‚ not now.    If someone goes on to hurt others\‚ that is on them and on the systems and adults who failed to step in\‚ not the child who was trying to survive it.    Stop asking survivors why they didn’t do more. Start asking why the people who could’ve done something… didn’t.    #victimblaming #abusesurvivor #believesurvivors #abuseawareness #breakthestigma

body: It’s easy to say\‚  “Why didn’t you report him?”    But when you’re in it\‚ it doesn’t feel that simple.    It doesn’t happen all at once.  It happens slowly.    You get hurt… and then things calm down.  He apologizes\‚ or acts normal again.  And your brain grabs onto that version of him because it feels safer than facing what just happened.    So instead of thinking\‚ “this is abuse\‚”  you’re thinking\‚ “that wasn’t really him… it won’t happen again.”    And you move on.    That’s how it gets minimized.  That’s how it gets buried.    I don’t regret much in my life.  But I regret not reporting what he did to me every time he did it.     Because now there’s no record.  No proof.  Nothing that says what happened to me was real.    And the hardest part…  other women won’t know either.    If you’ve ever stayed quiet about something you shouldn’t have had to endure\‚  you’re not alone.  But your story still matters.    Follow for more on breaking cycles and reclaiming your power.
author: herrestoredlife

body: “Why is she speaking out now?”  “Why did she leave it so late?”  “Why didn’t she say anything at the time?”    All harmless questions right? – ❌wrong.     They are part of a culture that disbelieves and silences survivors. One of the most common myths about rape\‚ is that If it had really happened\‚ then it wouldn’t have taken them so long to say something.    Yet the reality is\‚ 5 out of 6 women in the UK never report sexual violence\‚ and 90% of students don’t either.    I’ve teamed up with @rapecrisis.southlondon to explore some of the reasons\‚ myths and misconceptions around survivors’ silence. @rapecrisis.southlondon is Breaking the Silence: with specialist support for survivors\‚ and by challenging the systems that silence them in the first place. But they can’t do it alone.    Your donation helps keep these services free and accessible. You can support their work via the 🔗 in my bio☝️    AD  #breakingthesilence

body: You should never ask a child why they didn’t tell. It should be obvious. If not\‚ then please educate on how perpetrators abuse and what happens when children do tell. ⠀  ⠀  A child has to know what is happening to them is even considered abuse. They have to know they can ask for help. But\‚ it’s not just about knowledge- it’s also about risk.⠀  ⠀  Children that are being abused are very good at calculating their risk. They know how their parent will probably react if they tell. They may know what happened to another child that told. The child definitely knows what the abuser will do if they try to tell but are not believed and protected. ⠀  ⠀  If we want children to tell\‚ give them a reason they should. Believe\‚ protect\‚ and defend the child.  #whyididntreport #believeprotectdefend #defendsurvivors
author: defendsurvivors

body: This is why so many survivors stay silent.     Because “you didn’t say no” is still treated like a defence.    But consent is the presence of a clear\‚ voluntary yes \‚not the absence of a no.    When someone freezes\‚ their nervous system has shifted into survival mode. Fight\‚ flight\‚ freeze\‚ fawn \‚ these are automatic responses\‚ they are not choices.    Many people who are assaulted don’t scream\‚ fight or report. This does not mean it didn’t happen\‚ it means their body was trying to keep them safe.     I know this not just professionally \‚ but personally too. (I too froze and was scared to speak up)     It’s sad that in this day and age\‚ victims still fear being questioned more than they trust being protected. This is so wrong and needs to change.     A creative advertising team made this ad to highlight how the legal system sometimes fails.     ✨What are your thoughts?     Remix with:@wlthx  Cr: @calebjoehansen   @weridetogether.today

body: When someone speaks about sexual abuse years later\‚ the first question shouldn’t be:    “Why didn’t you report it sooner?”    Because the truth is — trauma doesn’t follow a timeline.    The brain prioritises survival\‚ not logic.  Understanding what happened can take time.  Feeling safe enough to speak can take even longer.    And for many\‚ silence isn’t a choice —  it’s the only way they knew how to cope at the time.    We need to shift the conversation away from blame and towards understanding.    Because every time we question why someone didn’t speak sooner\‚  we make it harder for the next person to speak at all.  @brandy     #doctoramirkhan #doctoramir #safeguardingwomen

body: Why is it that when someone speaks up\‚ the first questions are about what they did… instead of what was done to them?    Why didn’t you fight back? Why were you there? Why didn’t you leave? Why didn’t you report straight away? Why didn’t you scream?     But rarely: why did someone choose to harm another person?    Your body has no control in that moment. It goes into survival mode whether that looks like fighting\‚ freezing\‚ or shutting down. You are doing whatever you can to survive.    Victim blaming shifts the focus away from the person responsible and puts it onto the person who lived through it. It’s easier for people to question survivors than to face the truth.    Let’s be clear: the only person to blame is the one who committed the sexual violence.     #foryou #mentalhealth #sexualviolence #trending #domesticabuse

body: So\‚ I didn’t know what to share today\‚ but I was inspired (by another post I feel to be problematic) to re-share the fourth part of my mini series about rape myths. See original post below.    Part 4 in a series of information slides I’m making to try and create awareness and generate conversations around the types of rape myths that exist and the harm they cause for the victim. This can often lead to perpetrators not being prosecuted and victims being blamed and re-traumatised by this.     Today’s post highlights the facts about false reports and discredits the idea that women “cry rape” when they regret having sex with a man or as a means of getting revenge.    Please feel free to share\‚ save\‚ or use as discussion points.    Information taken from the Crown Prosecution Service website (www.cps.gov.uk: Rape and Sexual Offences: Chapter 21 - Societal Myths)\‚ “Fact Check: the truth about false rape claims” (2018)\‚ and “Here’s the truth about false accusations of sexual violence” (Lazard\‚ 2017).    #sexualassault #sexualviolence #sexual #assault #violence #violenceagainstwomen #women #violenceprevention #rapeculture #rapemyths #rapeawareness #rapevictim #victim #rapevictimsupport #rapevictimrecovery #abuse #victimsupprt #trauma #traumarecovery #traumahealing #traumainformed #whyididntreport #believethem #falsereport #believethevictim #holdperpetratorsaccountable #psychology #psychologist  #psychologistsofinstagram #forensicpsychology

body: Survivors of SA & other trauma often feel shame & guilt associated with their experiences.     For me\‚ part of the shame was the fact that I had been apart of something completely disgusting and embarrassing.     I didn’t want people to know that those things happened to me and that’s one of the biggest reasons I didn’t report for so long.     I knew that if I reported\‚ I would have to tell someone in explicit detail what happened to me\‚ what he did to me\‚ and what he made me do to him.     As a man\‚ who was violated by another man\‚ (even though I was just a boy) it was incredibly disturbing and embarrassing on another level.     #shame #survivor #traumahealing #sasurvivor #mentalhealth

body: Let\\'s talk about Rape False Allegations! Time to listen to this amazing and educational video by @lamujermorena.public     And let\\'s get some facts right;    5% of rapes are falsely reported worldwide\‚ the same percentage as other felonies.    But that figure also paints a very incomplete picture:    Typically\‚ this figure comes from studies done on college students\‚ an estimated 95 percent of whom do not report their assaults to the police.     Overall\‚ an estimated 2 - 8% percent of women are thought to report their rapes to the police\‚ which means that — at the very highest — we can infer that 90 percent of rapes go unreported.    Obviously\‚ only those rapes that are reported in the first place can be considered falsely reported. So that 5 percent figure only applies to 10 percent (at most) of rapes that occur.     This puts the actual false allegation figure closer to 0.5 percent!     #believeher #womenforchange  #metoo #believesurvivors #nomore #EnoughisEnough #WhyIDidntSpeakUp #whyididntreport

body: ⚠️ TW: trauma\‚ sex*al ass*ult\‚ stalking⚠️  [in sequence: me at 14\‚ 15\‚ 16\‚ 17 y/o]    Despite there being different schools of thought on that \\fact\\" [first pic]\‚ ever since I got r*ped (ig censorship?) I held on tightly to that hope. In 7 years I would\\'ve become \\"new\\"\‚ \\"clean\\".   But it\\'s actually been 10 years since my first trauma\‚ and even though I carried the weight of the \\"bad feelings\\" associated to that place and person\‚ I truly realized what happened and how I could name that only a couple of months ago.   At 14 I survived attempted r*pe.   That was my first sexual experience ever. And it\\'s going to be quite a long list of firsts.   But I still need to work on that in order to be able to openly talk about it.   At 15 I got molested by a 19 y/o guy who was tutoring me\‚ at my own house. He later (at 16) became my stalker\‚ after I managed to fire him and block him on each and every social platform\‚ but that didn\\'t stop him from reaching out to all my fb contacts\‚ finding my neighbor (who ofc is not to blame cause she didn\\'t know anything about it) and asking her to set a trap for me so he would finally be able to meet me irl again.  And despite quitting his stalking after I threatened to report him\‚ he had the audacity to \\"super like\\" me on Tinder a couple of years later\‚ as if nothing happened.  And this leads to me\‚ not being able to have any sexual/intimate relationship\‚ so I was still a virgin at 17 y/o -yeah social construct\‚ whatever- (I take this opportunity to apologize to anyone whom I\\'ve ever lied to\‚ I think you can understand now).  In August 2014\‚ I was r*ped (most likely also drugged) by two family friends.   To whomever blames victims for not reporting the crimes (at all or \\"in time\\") I say: I spent the first year after this as a zombie. Completely dissociated. And to top it all\‚ I was already also dealing with self harm\‚ depression\‚ anorexia and suicidal thoughts.   [I\\'ll continue in the comments]    #healingfromabuse #believesurvivors #healingsexualtrauma #rapevictim #traumainformed #rapeisrape #selflovemovement #healingfromtheinsideout #healingtrauma #mentalhealthawareness #nomorerape #whyididntreport #rapesurvivor #nomoreshame #endrapeculturenow"
